perturbation.original_text: Subject to the direction and control of the Board of Trustees of the Trust, the Sponsor shall perform such oversight, administrative and management services as may from time to time be reasonably requested by the Trust, which shall include without limitation: (a) maintaining office facilities (which may be in the office of Domini or an affiliate) and furnishing clerical services necessary for maintaining the organization of the Trust and for performing the oversight, administrative and management functions herein set forth; (b) arranging, if desired by the Trust, for directors, officers or employees of the Sponsor to serve as Trustees, officers or agents of the Trust if duly elected or appointed to such positions and subject to their individual consent and to any limitations imposed by law; (c) supervising the overall administration of the Trust, including the updating of corporate organizational documents, and the negotiation of contracts and fees with and the monitoring and coordinating of performance and billings of the Trust's transfer agent, shareholder servicing agents (if any), custodian, administrator, subadministrator (if any) and other independent contractors or agents; (d) overseeing (with advice of the Trust's counsel) the preparation of and, if applicable, filing all documents required for compliance by the Trust with applicable laws and regulations (including state "blue sky" laws and regulations), including registration statements on Form N-1A, prospectuses and statements of additional information, or similar forms, as applicable, semi-annual and annual reports to shareholders and proxy statements, and reviewing tax returns; (e) preparation of agendas and supporting documents for and minutes of meetings of Trustees, committees of Trustees and preparation of notices, proxy statements and minutes of meetings of shareholders; (f) arranging for maintenance of books and records of the Trust; (g) maintaining telephone coverage to respond to shareholder inquiries regarding matters to which this Agreement pertains to which the transfer agent is unable to respond; (h) providing reports and assistance regarding each series' compliance with securities and tax laws and each series' investment objectives; (i) arranging for dissemination of yield and other performance information to newspapers and tracking services; (j) arranging for and preparing annual renewals for fidelity bond and errors and omissions insurance coverage; (k) developing a budget for the Trust, establishing the rate of expense accruals and arranging for the payment of all fixed and management expenses; and (l) answering questions from the general public, the media and investors in the Trust regarding (i) the securities holdings of the Trust; (ii) any limits in which the Trust invests; (iii) the social investment philosophy of the Trust; and (iv) the proxy voting philosophy and shareholder activism philosophy of the Trust.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the direction and control of the Board of Trustees of the Trust, the Sponsor shall endeavor to perform such oversight, administrative and management services as may from time to time be suggested by the Trust, which shall include, when feasible: (a) maintaining suitable office facilities and furnishing such clerical services as are convenient for maintaining the organization of the Trust; (b) considering arranging for directors, officers or employees of the Sponsor to serve as Trustees, officers or agents of the Trust if asked; (c) assisting with the overall administration of the Trust, and the negotiation of contracts and fees; (d) generally monitoring the preparation of documents for compliance with applicable laws and regulations, and reviewing tax returns; (e) assisting in the preparation of agendas and minutes of meetings; (f) aiding in the maintenance of books and records of the Trust; (g) maintaining reasonable telephone coverage; (h) providing reports and assistance regarding each series' investment objectives; (i) arranging for dissemination of yield and other performance information; (j) arranging for renewals for fidelity bond and errors and omissions insurance coverage; (k) developing a budget for the Trust and arranging for the payment of expenses; and (l) addressing questions from the general public regarding the Trust.
perturbation.explanation: The changes replace mandatory 'shall' with weaker 'endeavor to' and 'assisting,' and replace precise requirements (e.g., maintaining office facilities) with vague concepts (e.g., suitable office facilities) making the sponsor's obligations less concrete and enforceable. This weakens the Sponsor's duties and gives them more discretion, potentially leading to non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires that investment advisory contracts, which this sponsorship agreement arguably falls under, be specific about the services to be provided. By weakening the language to be non-mandatory with the new language, the contract becomes ambiguous about the Sponsor's actual responsibilities, potentially violating the requirement for clear delineation of duties under the Investment Company Act of 1940.

Contracts of advisers and underwriters (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the Sponsor's obligations, creating a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation.' The original contract uses 'shall,' indicating mandatory duties. The changed text replaces 'shall' with weaker terms like 'endeavor to,' 'assisting,' and 'considering,' transforming obligations into suggestions or possibilities. This makes the Sponsor's responsibilities less enforceable and contradicts the need for clarity in investment advisory contracts as suggested by the Law Explanation, where it states that Section 15(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires that investment advisory contracts 'be specific about the services to be provided.' Snippet 1 mentions '80a-15 : Contracts of advisers and underwriters' which refers to '§80a–15. Contracts of advisers and underwriters (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract...'

perturbation.original_text: The Trust shall pay all of its operating expenses, including but not limited to fees due the Sponsor under this Agreement, compensation of Trustees not affiliated with the Sponsor, governmental fees, including but not limited to Securities and Exchange Commission fees and state "blue sky" fees; interest charges; taxes and related charges; membership dues of the Trust in the Investment Company Institute and other professional or industry associations; fees and expenses of the Trust's independent auditors and accountants, of legal counsel and any transfer agent, distributor, shareholder servicing agent, recordkeeper, registrar or dividend disbursing agent of the Trust; expenses of distributing, issuing and redeeming shares and servicing shareholder accounts; expenses of preparing, printing and mailing prospectuses and statements of additional information, reports, notices, proxy statements and reports to shareholders and governmental officers and commissions; expenses connected with the execution, recording and settlement of portfolio security transactions; insurance premiums; fees and expenses of the Trust's custodian for all services to the Trust, including safekeeping of funds and securities and maintaining required books and accounts; expenses of calculating the net asset value of shares of the Trust; expenses of shareholder meetings; and expenses relating to the issuance, registration and qualification of shares of any series of the Trust.
perturbation.changed_text: The Trust shall generally be responsible for its operating expenses, potentially including fees due the Sponsor, compensation of unaffiliated Trustees, governmental fees, interest charges, taxes, membership dues, fees and expenses of auditors, legal counsel, transfer agents, distributors, shareholder servicing agents, recordkeepers, registrars, or dividend disbursing agents; expenses of distributing, issuing, and redeeming shares; expenses of preparing, printing, and mailing prospectuses, reports, notices, and proxy statements; expenses connected with portfolio security transactions; insurance premiums; custodian fees; expenses of calculating net asset value; expenses of shareholder meetings; and expenses relating to the issuance, registration, and qualification of shares.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall pay all' to 'shall generally be responsible' introduces ambiguity about which expenses the Trust is definitively obligated to cover. Adding 'potentially including' makes the list of expenses non-exhaustive and discretionary. This ambiguity could lead to disputes over expense allocation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 prohibits certain affiliated persons from engaging in transactions with the investment company. Ambiguous language regarding who pays for what can create a situation where expenses are unfairly allocated in favor of the sponsor (an affiliated person) and against the interests of the fund and its shareholders, thus potentially violating this section.

Transactions of certain affiliated persons and underwriters (a) Prohibited transactions It shall be unlawful for any affiliated person or promoter of or principal underwriter for a registered investment company (other than a company of the character described in section 80a–12(d)(3)(A) and (B) of this title),
perturbation.justification: The original text states "The Trust shall pay all of its operating expenses," creating a clear obligation. The changed text introduces ambiguity by stating "The Trust shall generally be responsible for its operating expenses, potentially including fees...", weakening the obligation and potentially violating Section 17(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940. As the Law Explanation states, ambiguous language can lead to unfair expense allocation favoring the sponsor and harming the fund's interests, potentially violating the law. The change from 'shall pay all' to 'shall generally be responsible' creates an ambiguous legal obligation, contradicting the initial clear obligation and creating the potential for affiliate abuse. This aligns with the definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction', making the legal obligation difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: Domini may subcontract for the performance of some or all of Domini's obligations hereunder with any one or more persons; provided, however, that Domini shall not enter into any such subcontract unless the Trustees of the Trust shall have found the subcontracting party to be qualified to perform the obligations sought to be subcontracted; and provided, further, that, unless the Trust otherwise expressly agrees in writing, Domini shall be as fully responsible to the Trust for the acts and omissions of any subcontractor as it would be for its own acts or omissions.
perturbation.changed_text: Domini may consider subcontracting some of its obligations with other persons; provided that Domini believes that the subcontracting party has the basic qualifications to perform the obligations; and provided, unless otherwise agreed, Domini will generally be responsible for the actions of any subcontractor.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'may subcontract' to 'may consider subcontracting' makes subcontracting optional. Changing 'shall have found the subcontracting party to be qualified' to 'Domini believes that the subcontracting party has the basic qualifications' lowers the standard of due diligence. Replacing 'shall be as fully responsible' with 'will generally be responsible' weakens the liability provision. These changes give Domini more leeway in subcontracting and reduce the protections for the Trust.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 206 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 establishes fiduciary duties for investment advisers. By weakening the language related to subcontracting, there is a higher potential for the sponsor to select subcontractors who are not qualified to perform the necessary responsibilities for the Trust. This could violate the investment advisor's fiduciary duty to the Trust.

Prohibited transactions by investment advisers It shall be unlawful for any investment adviser by use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, directly or indirectly- (1) to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client; (2) to engage in any transaction, practice, or course of business which
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates Domini to ensure subcontracting parties are 'qualified' via Trustee evaluation, and holds Domini 'fully responsible' for subcontractor actions. The changed text, however, only requires Domini to 'believe' in 'basic qualifications' of subcontractors and makes Domini 'generally responsible'. According to the Law Explanation, Section 206 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 establishes fiduciary duties for investment advisers. The alteration weakens subcontractor selection criteria and liability, increasing the risk of unqualified subcontractors, thus potentially breaching the investment advisor's fiduciary duty to the Trust. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Prohibited transactions by investment advisers' and using 'any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client'. By weakening the due diligence and liability, the altered contract creates an ambiguity which could be a device or scheme to defraud.
